chunk_summary: This section emphasizes diet-first strategies for microbiome modulation in children, focusing on breastfeeding, complementary feeding, and prebiotic food diversity while minimizing unnecessary supplements and interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of diet-first approaches in microbiome health for children across different age groups.
Identify age-appropriate prebiotic and fermented food options for children under 2 years.
Recognize when to use prebiotic or synbiotic supplements under specialist supervision.
Learn strategies to prevent and manage constipation and diarrhea in children.
key_facts: Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for infants under 6 months to support microbiome health.
For children aged 6–23 months, WHO complementary feeding principles should be followed, including introducing prebiotic foods like pulses, oats, bananas, and fermented foods like yogurt.
Daily fiber intake and minimally processed meals are recommended for children aged 2 years and older, using staples like dal, whole grains, and seasonal fruits.
Prebiotic supplements or synbiotics should only be used under specialist supervision for specific indications and discontinued if no clinical improvement is observed.
Antibiotic exposure should be minimized, and diarrhea should be treated with ORS and zinc rather than microbiome restoration products.
reference_quote: Guideline for complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months of age — https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240081864
document_key_topics: Role of prebiotic and fermented foods in gut health
